text: The surgeon uses the DaVinci XI robotic platform to tuck the liver above the stomach, providing clear access for the procedure. The hiatus, where the esophagus meets the stomach, is inspected, revealing a small hiatal hernia. Fat is cleaned from around the area, and the stomach is tucked and pulled back into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to carefully free loose tissue around the hiatal hernia. The device simultaneously seals and cuts vessels to prevent bleeding. An opening through the diaphragm into the chest is visible, indicating the stomach's migration due to the hernia.

The surgeon performs circumferential dissection around the esophagus as it enters the abdominal cavity. The white structure above the vessel sealer is identified as the vagus nerve, which is lifted and protected by the surgeon's left hand. Loose tissue and fat are cleaned from the back of the esophagus, revealing a sizable opening.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer device to tie and cut the blood supply along the greater curvature of the stomach, ensuring all small blood vessels are ligated as they approach the hiatus.

The surgeon uses a suture to close the defect, visibly reducing its size. A tube is passed through the esophagus to ensure the closure is not too tight, preventing swallowing issues. The tube is then used to size the new stomach, passing close to the pylorus to ensure consistent removal of the necessary stomach portion.

The surgeon uses a stapler with three rows of sutures to transect and cut the stomach, moving sequentially towards the hiatus. A tube is visible on the left side of the screen, serving as a sizer to ensure the correct amount of stomach is removed. After transection, the right portion of the stomach is removed, and the new stomach is submerged underwater for an air leak test. The anesthesiologist injects air to confirm the airtightness of the suture line. The old stomach appears grayish due to the removal of its blood supply and is set for removal.
